sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=55, col=30).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 44, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 43 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Center cell turned green (N). All other cells are gray. So clicking a gray cell turns it green. This is the same behavior as before.
Now I have only the center cell green. Let me think about what pattern the left panel shows:
- Purple dots at: center, top, left, right (cross pattern)
- No dot at bottom
Wait, let me look at the left panel again. The purple dots form a cross pattern with 4 dots. Looking at the image, I see:
- One dot in the center
- One dot above center
- One dot to the left of center
- One dot to the right of center
That's a "T" shape or an upside-down cross (missing the bottom arm).
But the grid is 3x3. The cross pattern on the grid has 5 cells: top-center, middle-left, center, middle-right, bottom-center.
The left panel has 4 purple dots. Maybe the goal is to have exactly 4 green cells in the cross pattern, matching the purple dot positions?
Looking at the left panel more carefully:
- (51, 15) - center of left panel
- (54, 12) - left of center
- (54, 18) - right of center  
- (57, 15) - below center
Wait, that's not a cross pattern. Let me re-examine. The left panel is a 5x5 area. The purple dots are at:
- (51, 15) - row 51, col 15 - this is the top-center of the panel
- (54, 12) - row 54, col 12 - this is the middle-left
- (54, 18) - row 54, col 18 - this is the middle-right
- (57, 15) - row 57, col 15 - this is the bottom-center
So the pattern is: top, left, right, bottom (a cross without the center). This matches the 4 cross cells on the grid (excluding the center).
So the goal might be to have the 4 cross cells (excluding center) be green, and the center be gray. That's exactly what I had before they all turned gray!
But when I clicked all 4 cross cells, they all turned gray. Let me try a different approach: click the purple dot to get white cross, then click the 4 cross cells one at a time, checking after each.
Wait, I already tried this and it worked for 2 cells but then clicking the 3rd and 4th caused all to turn gray. Let me try clicking them one at a time with separate action calls to see exactly when the reset happens.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is NOT to have the cross cells green. Maybe the goal is something else entirely.
Let me look at the upper bar more carefully. The blue/sky blue blocks on the right side have been growing. Maybe they represent progress, and when they reach a certain size, the level completes.
Let me try clicking the sky blue block on the right side of the bar.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me track the bar blocks across history to understand their behavior
# Also try clicking the sky blue block on the right side of the bar
# First, let me check the current bar state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Bar-related nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('b', 'S') and n['pixels'] <= 20:
        bnd = n['boundary']
        r = sum(p[0] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        c = sum(p[1] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({r},{c}), hash={n['hash']}")

# Try clicking the sky blue block on the right side of the bar
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 19, 'col': 37}])
print(f"\nLevel completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("Bar-related nodes after click:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('b', 'S') and n['pixels'] <= 20:
        bnd = n['boundary']
        r = sum(p[0] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        c = sum(p[1] for p in bnd) // len(bnd)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({r},{c})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
